record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Superior Court (county-level marriage licenses from territorial era)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1909 (state vital records registration; earlier records scattered in county, church, and territorial records)

burned_county_authority: Arizona State Library, Archives & Public Records (ASLAPR); Arizona has no formally designated 'burned counties' — most record losses relate to territorial-era gaps, floods, and county seat relocations rather than courthouse fires
administrative_notes: Arizona was the 48th state, admitted February 14, 1912 — the last of the contiguous states. Arizona Territory was created in 1863 from the western half of New Mexico Territory. The region was acquired from Mexico through the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848) and the Gadsden Purchase (1853). Four original territorial counties were created in 1864: Mohave, Pima, Yavapai, and Yuma. Arizona has 22 federally recognized tribal nations — more than any other state except Alaska — whose lands span multiple counties and whose records are maintained separately by tribal governments. Spanish colonial records (missions, presidios) date to the 1690s in southern Arizona. Mormon settlement in eastern Arizona from the 1870s-1880s created distinctive communities with extensive LDS church records.

core_repositories.source_name: Arizona State Library, Archives & Public Records (ASLAPR)
core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for Arizona. Houses territorial government records, microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, probate), vital records, prison records, naturalization records, and state agency archives. The Archives Division holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps. Online catalog and some digitized collections available.

State historical society with four branches. Tucson headquarters holds extensive manuscript collections, photographs, oral histories, and published materials. Tempe branch covers central Arizona history. Flagstaff branch covers northern Arizona. Yuma branch covers Colorado River region. Essential for family and community history research.

Outstanding territorial-era research facility in the original Governor's Mansion. Holdings include territorial governor papers, early newspaper files, manuscript collections, and Prescott area records. Named for Sharlot Hall, Arizona's first territorial historian.

Extensive holdings in southwestern history including Spanish colonial documents, mining records, ranching papers, and Arizona territorial records. Also holds Tucson/Pima County community records and photographs.

core_repositories.source_name: NAU Cline Library Special Collections and Archives
core_repositories.description: Specializes in Colorado Plateau history: lumber industry, railroad, ranching, and Native American records. Strong holdings for Coconino, Navajo, and Apache county areas. Includes oral histories and photographs.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, probate), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and LDS church records. Extensive microfilm partnership with ASLAPR and county offices. Critical for Arizona research — many county records microfilmed and accessible online.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, territorial-era research strategies, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes guides to LDS church records in Arizona.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Arizona newspapers from territorial era forward. Includes the Arizona Miner (Prescott, 1864), Tombstone Epitaph, Arizona Daily Star, Arizona Republican, and smaller county papers. Essential for obituaries, legal notices, and community events.
Microfilmed Arizona newspapers not in Chronicling America. Holdings include territorial-era papers, mining camp newspapers, and small-town weeklies from across the state.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Navajo Nation (Dine) is the largest reservation in the US, spanning Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah. Tribal records include enrollment, allotment, grazing permits, and community records. Access controlled by tribal government. Navajo Nation Historic Preservation Department handles cultural records.

Hopi tribal records including enrollment and community documents. The Hopi reservation is entirely surrounded by the Navajo Nation in northeastern Arizona. Access to cultural records is restricted by the tribe.

Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Arizona agencies: Navajo, Pima, Colorado River, Fort Apache, San Carlos, Papago (Tohono O'odham), and others. Census rolls, allotment records, school records (Phoenix Indian School, Keams Canyon), and correspondence. NARA Record Group 75.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: San Carlos Apache and White Mountain Apache Tribal Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: San Carlos Apache Tribe / White Mountain Apache Tribe
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Tribal enrollment records, allotment records, and community documents for the San Carlos and Fort Apache (White Mountain) reservations in eastern Arizona. Access controlled by respective tribal governments.

The Tohono O'odham Nation (formerly Papago) holds the second-largest reservation in the US. Tribal enrollment, allotment, and community records. The nation spans the US-Mexico border; some O'odham communities are in Sonora, Mexico.

Phoenix Indian School (1891-1990) student records including enrollment, attendance, health, and correspondence. Students came from tribes across Arizona and the Southwest. Records at NARA Riverside (RG 75) and some at ASLAPR.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Arizona Territorial and Federal Naturalization Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization proceedings from Arizona territorial courts (1863-1912) and federal courts. Significant for mining communities with large immigrant populations (Mexican, Cornish, Italian, Slavic, Chinese). ASLAPR holds microfilmed territorial court records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Nogales and Douglas Border Crossing Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: US-Mexico border crossing manifests and records from Nogales, Douglas, Naco, and other Arizona ports of entry. Essential for Mexican immigration research. Includes manifests of aliens arriving (1906-1954) and other INS records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files — Arizona
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Case files for Chinese residents and immigrants in Arizona under the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882-1943). Documents Chinese communities in Tucson, Phoenix, Tombstone, and mining towns. Includes photographs, affidavits, and community information.

military_records.description: WWI draft registration cards, WWII service records, and Arizona National Guard records. Also holds records of territorial militia and Arizona Volunteer Infantry (Spanish-American War).

Post returns and records from Arizona forts: Whipple, Huachuca, McDowell, Apache, Thomas, Yuma, Bowie, Grant, Lowell, and others. Indian campaign records, Buffalo Soldier records, and garrison records. NARA Record Group 393 and 94.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Arizona
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Arizona: homestead entries, desert land entries, mineral entries, and timber culture claims. Searchable online database with document images. Critical for tracing original settlement on public domain lands.

land_and_property.source_name: Court of Private Land Claims Records (1891-1904)
land_and_property.description: Adjudicated Spanish and Mexican land grants in Arizona. The Court of Private Land Claims (based in Tucson) confirmed or rejected claims to lands granted before the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848) and Gadsden Purchase (1853). Case files include testimony, maps, and chain of title.

vital_records.source_name: Arizona Department of Health Services — Vital Records
vital_records.repository: AZ Dept of Health Services, Phoenix
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1909 (statewide registration). Marriage and divorce records from county level. For pre-1909 vital events, check county records (Clerk of Superior Court), church registers, cemetery records, and territorial-era documents. Historical records (100+ years for births, 75+ for deaths) may be available through ASLAPR.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Diocese of Tucson — Catholic Church Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic church records for southern Arizona including mission records from San Xavier del Bac (1692), Tumacacori, Guevavi, and later parish registers. Baptisms, marriages, and burials for O'odham, Yaqui, Hispanic, and Anglo Catholic families. Some records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Diocese of Phoenix — Catholic Church Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic parish records for central and northern Arizona. Diocese established 1969 from Diocese of Tucson. Earlier records in Tucson diocese.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Arizona. Coverage varies by county — well-covered urban cemeteries versus sparse rural and reservation coverage. Pioneer cemeteries in Phoenix, Prescott, and Tucson have extensive records.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes journal and maintains research resources. Hosts seminars and workshops on Arizona genealogy.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, and county-specific research guides for all 15 Arizona counties.

Digital collections from Arizona libraries, archives, and museums. Includes photographs, maps, documents, and oral histories from across the state. Useful for visual identification and community history.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Arizona has no abolished or extinct counties. However, the territory's boundaries and county configurations changed significantly: (1) Before 1863, the area was part of New Mexico Territory — records from 1850-1863 are in New Mexico territorial records at NARA and New Mexico State Records Center. (2) The four original counties (1864) — Mohave, Pima, Yavapai, and Yuma — were the parents of all subsequent counties. (3) Pah-Ute County existed briefly (1865-1871) covering the Arizona Strip area north of the Colorado River; it was transferred to Nevada and then to Utah. Records may be in Lincoln County, Nevada, or Washington County, Utah. (4) Several mining towns and communities became ghost towns (La Paz, Ehrenberg, Oatman, Jerome partially) — their records may be scattered across multiple repositories.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Arizona has no formally designated 'burned counties' in the traditional genealogical sense. Record losses are generally limited: (1) Mohave County lost some early records through multiple county seat relocations (1864-1887). (2) Greenlee County (Clifton) experienced flood damage in 1906 and 1916. (3) Some territorial-era records are sparse due to the frontier nature of early Arizona. (4) Spanish colonial and Mexican-era records for southern Arizona are scattered across multiple repositories in the US and Mexico. Overall, Arizona's county records are remarkably well preserved compared to many southeastern states.
